g.text: Adagio for Strings
, by Samuel Barber
One of the enduring works of the twentieth century,
Adagio for Strings
has been called
"America's semi-oﬃcial music for mourning" and featured in many movies and television
shows. Written in 1938, it is a string orchestra adaptation of the second movement of his
String Quartet, Op. 11.
For Bidirectional Unmetered Mixed ensemble
This particular arrangement of
Adagio for Strings
erases a signiﬁcant amount of detail
from the original in order to make a particular claim: that the architectural beauty of the
music is appreciable backwards as well as forwards. To prove this claim, the arranger has
taken "snapshots" of the voice-leading and folded them into a chord. This chord is
presented in modiﬁed western notation as a treble voice, a middle voice, and bass voice.
Navigating the arrangement
The web app running this session has two modes, "play" and "guide". Touching "guide",
and then either of the colored rectangles, will initiate a voting period during which all
participants may vote forward (beige) or backward (lime green). Folks may also vote to
"stay" on the present frame. Because the group only encounters one frame at a time,
there is no "getting lost".
While the melismatic signature is absent from the left and right measures, harmonic
motion remains present. The melisma is preserved in the central measure, although
device playback treats it as a tone cluster, and the live experience treats it as an
asynchronous (or optional) event.
Note on the clefs, 1: an "octave above bass clef" has been chosen for the middle voice.
Note on the clefs, 2: The right side of the frame shows reversed clefs, reminding the
participant at each frame of the bidirectional conceit of this arrangement.
